---
title: "Gram–Schmidt and the QR Decomposition"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

POINT  = "#2b3a67"    # data points / generic vectors
ARROW  = "#c1121f"    # the highlighted arrow / first direction
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"    # second orthogonal direction / highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PURPLE = "#6a4c93"    # third direction / residual

def gram_schmidt(A, modified=False):
    """Return (Q, R) for the thin QR via Gram–Schmidt — the very process this
    article derives, used here only to draw the pictures and run the checks.
    Classical when modified=False, modified Gram–Schmidt when True."""
    A = np.asarray(A, dtype=float)
    m, n = A.shape
    Q = np.zeros((m, n))
    R = np.zeros((n, n))
    for j in range(n):
        v = A[:, j].copy()
        if not modified:
            # classical: project the ORIGINAL column onto each finished q
            for i in range(j):
                R[i, j] = Q[:, i] @ A[:, j]
                v = v - R[i, j] * Q[:, i]
        else:
            # modified: subtract each projection as we go, from the running v
            for i in range(j):
                R[i, j] = Q[:, i] @ v
                v = v - R[i, j] * Q[:, i]
        R[j, j] = np.linalg.norm(v)
        Q[:, j] = v / R[j, j]
    return Q, R
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the seventh article in the **Geometry of
> Data** track and the immediate sequel to the keystone. It assumes
> [GEOM-5 *Orthogonality and Orthonormal Bases*] — the dot product as a right-angle test
> $\mathbf x^\top\mathbf y = 0$, the **orthonormal basis** in which a coordinate is just a
> dot product $c_i = \mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf x$, and the **orthogonal decomposition**
> $\mathbf x = (\text{part in the subspace}) + (\text{part perpendicular to it})$ — and
> above all [GEOM-6 *Orthogonal Projection onto a Subspace*], which built the single move
> we repeat over and over here: the projection of a vector onto a direction, and the
> guarantee that the **residual is orthogonal** to what we projected onto. This article
> turns that one move into a *machine*: feed it any basis, and it manufactures an
> orthonormal one for the same space, bookkeeping the work into the factorization
> $A = QR$. It sets up [GEOM-11 *Solving $A\mathbf x = \mathbf b$*] and
> [GEOM-12 *Rank and the Four Subspaces*] (where QR becomes the numerically sound way to
> solve least squares), and [GEOM-26 *Condition Number and Numerical Stability*] (where
> the "orthogonality decays" warning we raise here is paid off in full). It is the route
> Regression is handed for a stable least-squares solve — the honest alternative to the
> normal equations of [REGR-1].

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Orthonormal bases are the most pleasant coordinate systems in linear algebra — in one,
every coordinate is a single dot product and projection collapses to a sum of those dot
products (GEOM-5, GEOM-6). But data never arrives orthonormal; it arrives as whatever
columns we happened to measure. **Gram–Schmidt** is the constructive bridge: given any
linearly independent list $\mathbf a_1,\dots,\mathbf a_n$, it produces an orthonormal list
$\mathbf q_1,\dots,\mathbf q_n$ spanning exactly the same subspaces, one vector at a time,
by the single rule *subtract off the part that already lies in the span so far, then
normalize*. We derive that rule from the projection of GEOM-6, prove at every step that it
does what it claims, and then notice that the arithmetic we did was secretly a matrix
factorization: $A = QR$, with $Q$ holding the orthonormal vectors and $R$ upper-triangular
holding the bookkeeping. The single claim this article defends is that **$QR$ is not a
separate algorithm bolted onto Gram–Schmidt — it *is* Gram–Schmidt, written down** — and
that once you have it, projection onto the column space of $A$ becomes the almost
embarrassingly simple $P = QQ^\top$. We close with the honest catch: the *classical*
version of the process loses orthogonality on a computer, and one tiny reordering — the
*modified* version — largely fixes it, our first real encounter with the numerical-reality
theme that closes the track.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Take any basis $\mathbf a_1,\dots,\mathbf a_n$. Walk through it one vector at a time. For
each new $\mathbf a_k$, **subtract off its projection onto everything you have already
orthogonalized**, leaving only the part pointing in a genuinely new direction; then scale
that part to unit length. The result is an **orthonormal basis** $\mathbf q_1,\dots,\mathbf
q_n$ for the same space. Record, in an upper-triangular matrix $R$, the projection
coefficients you subtracted and the lengths you divided by, and you have written
$A = QR$ — the **QR decomposition**, the orthonormal basis and the recipe that built it,
packaged together.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The whole process is one move from GEOM-6 — *project,
then keep the residual* — applied in a disciplined order. The bookkeeping that turns a
sequence of these moves into the clean equation $A=QR$ will look like an accounting trick
at first, but by the end you should see it as inevitable: the upper-triangular shape of
$R$ is nothing but the statement *"$\mathbf q_k$ was built only from
$\mathbf a_1,\dots,\mathbf a_k$, never from later columns."*

# Why we are doing this: the problem that forced the process {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the entire article: **we know orthonormal bases are golden
(GEOM-5) — so how do we actually get one, starting from the un-special vectors data hands
us?** Every step below is an answer to a piece of that question.

## What GEOM-5 promised, and the bill it left unpaid

In [GEOM-5] we established why an **orthonormal basis** — a set of mutually perpendicular
unit vectors $\mathbf q_1,\dots,\mathbf q_n$ with

$$
\mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf q_j =
\begin{cases} 1 & i = j \\ 0 & i \neq j \end{cases}
$$ {#eq-orthonormal}

— is the coordinate system you would design if you could. Two payoffs made it golden, and
we restate them because the entire motivation for this article is to *manufacture* the
bases that deliver them:

1. **Coordinates are free.** To write a vector $\mathbf x$ in an orthonormal basis you do
   not solve a linear system; each coordinate is a single dot product, $c_i = \mathbf
   q_i^\top\mathbf x$. (Derived in GEOM-5: dot $\mathbf x = \sum_j c_j\mathbf q_j$ with
   $\mathbf q_i$ and all cross-terms vanish by @eq-orthonormal.)
2. **Projection collapses.** Projecting a vector onto the span of orthonormal vectors is
   just adding up those coordinate pieces — no matrix inverse, no normal equations.

But GEOM-5 never told us where such a basis *comes from*. Real data does not arrive
orthonormal. A design matrix's columns are whatever predictors you measured — height,
weight, age — and they are correlated, of different lengths, and generally at oblique
angles to one another. The standard basis $\mathbf e_1,\dots,\mathbf e_n$ is orthonormal,
but it spans the *coordinate axes*, not the subspace your data actually lives in. The
unpaid bill is exactly this:

> Given the arbitrary, oblique basis the world hands you, **construct** an orthonormal
> basis for the *same* subspace.

## Why "the same subspace" is the whole point

We could trivially produce an orthonormal set — just hand back $\mathbf e_1,\dots,\mathbf
e_n$. The constraint that makes the problem real is that the new basis must span the
**same space** as the old one, and more than that, must build it up in the same order: the
first $k$ new vectors must span exactly what the first $k$ old ones spanned. That order-
preserving requirement is what makes the bookkeeping triangular, and triangularity is what
makes the factorization useful for *solving* things downstream (@sec-solving). So the
target is precise:

$$
\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf q_1,\dots,\mathbf q_k\}
= \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf a_1,\dots,\mathbf a_k\}
\qquad\text{for every } k = 1,\dots,n.
$$ {#eq-nested-span}

## A note on history: a process older than its name

The procedure is named for Jørgen Pedersen Gram (1883) and Erhard Schmidt (1907), but
neither invented it from nothing. Laplace and Cauchy used the same successive-orthogonal-
ization idea decades earlier in the theory of least squares and in eliminating variables
from quadratic forms; Schmidt, working on integral equations in Hilbert's circle at
Göttingen, gave the clean iterative statement we still teach. The *why* is the same across
all of them and across this article: orthogonal pieces do not interfere, so a problem that
is tangled in an oblique basis falls apart into independent one-dimensional problems once
you orthogonalize. That is the recurring dividend — least squares, QR solves, eigenvalue
iterations, and the SVD ([GEOM-20]) all cash it.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why bother orthogonalizing at all — why not just solve in the original basis?"* Because
in an oblique basis the coordinates of a vector are **coupled**: changing how much of
$\mathbf a_1$ you use changes the best amount of $\mathbf a_2$, because they overlap. You
must solve a full linear system (the normal equations of GEOM-6) to untangle them. In an
orthonormal basis the coordinates are **decoupled** — each is its own independent dot
product — so the system becomes trivial. Orthogonalizing *up front* trades one hard
coupled solve for a sequence of easy independent ones, and (the payoff of @sec-solving)
leaves behind a triangular system you can solve by inspection.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any formula, watch the process run in the plane and then in space, because the
whole algorithm is visible to the eye.

**Two vectors in the plane.** You are given two arrows $\mathbf a_1,\mathbf a_2$ at some
oblique angle. You want two *perpendicular* arrows spanning the same plane.

- Keep the first direction: let $\mathbf q_1$ point along $\mathbf a_1$ (just shrink it to
  length one).
- Now look at $\mathbf a_2$. Part of it lies along $\mathbf q_1$ — that is the *shadow* of
  $\mathbf a_2$ on the $\mathbf q_1$ line, the projection from GEOM-6. The rest of
  $\mathbf a_2$ sticks out perpendicular to $\mathbf q_1$. **Throw away the shadow, keep
  what sticks out.** What remains is, by the residual-is-orthogonal guarantee of GEOM-6,
  exactly perpendicular to $\mathbf q_1$. Shrink it to length one; that is $\mathbf q_2$.

Two perpendicular unit arrows, same plane. @fig-gs-2d shows precisely this subtraction.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gs-2d
#| fig-cap: "Gram–Schmidt on two vectors in the plane. We keep $\\mathbf a_1$'s direction as $\\mathbf q_1$. The projection of $\\mathbf a_2$ onto $\\mathbf q_1$ (the dashed grey shadow) is the part of $\\mathbf a_2$ that is *redundant* — it points in a direction we already have. Subtracting it leaves the residual $\\mathbf v_2$ (purple), which the GEOM-6 guarantee makes exactly perpendicular to $\\mathbf q_1$. Normalizing $\\mathbf v_2$ gives $\\mathbf q_2$. The right angle between $\\mathbf q_1$ and $\\mathbf q_2$ is marked."
a1 = np.array([3.0, 1.0])
a2 = np.array([1.5, 2.6])
q1 = a1 / np.linalg.norm(a1)
proj = (q1 @ a2) * q1          # shadow of a2 on q1
v2 = a2 - proj                 # residual, perpendicular to q1
q2 = v2 / np.linalg.norm(v2)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6, 5.0))
def arrow(ax, vec, color, label, lw=2.4):
    ax.annotate("", xy=vec, xytext=(0, 0),
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw))
    ax.text(vec[0]*1.04, vec[1]*1.04, label, color=color, fontsize=12)

arrow(ax, a1, GREY, r"$\mathbf{a}_1$")
arrow(ax, a2, POINT, r"$\mathbf{a}_2$")
# shadow + the dropped perpendicular
ax.plot([0, proj[0]], [0, proj[1]], color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.6)
ax.text(proj[0]*0.5, proj[1]*0.5 - 0.18, "shadow", color=GREY, fontsize=9)
ax.plot([proj[0], a2[0]], [proj[1], a2[1]], color=PURPLE, ls=":", lw=1.6)
arrow(ax, v2, PURPLE, r"$\mathbf{v}_2=\mathbf{a}_2-\mathrm{shadow}$", lw=2.0)
arrow(ax, q1, ARROW, r"$\mathbf{q}_1$")
arrow(ax, q2, ACCENT, r"$\mathbf{q}_2$")
# right-angle marker between q1 and q2
s = 0.22
corner = s*q1 + s*q2
ax.plot([s*q1[0], corner[0]], [s*q1[1], corner[1]], color="k", lw=1.0)
ax.plot([s*q2[0], corner[0]], [s*q2[1], corner[1]], color="k", lw=1.0)
ax.set_xlim(-0.5, 3.4); ax.set_ylim(-0.5, 3.0)
ax.set_aspect("equal"); ax.set_title("Keep the direction, subtract the shadow")
plt.tight_layout()
```

**A third vector in space.** Now you are handed $\mathbf a_3$ in three dimensions and you
already have a perpendicular pair $\mathbf q_1,\mathbf q_2$ spanning a plane. Same idea,
one step bigger: $\mathbf a_3$ has a shadow *in that whole plane* — a piece built from both
$\mathbf q_1$ and $\mathbf q_2$. Subtract the entire planar shadow; what remains pokes
straight out of the plane, perpendicular to it. Normalize, and you have $\mathbf q_3$. The
pattern is now clear and it never changes: **at step $k$, subtract the shadow on the span
of everything before, keep the perpendicular leftover, normalize.** @fig-gs-3d shows the
plane and the vector poking out of it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-gs-3d
#| fig-cap: "The inductive step in three dimensions. With $\\mathbf q_1,\\mathbf q_2$ already orthonormal and spanning the grey plane, the new vector $\\mathbf a_3$ (navy) casts a shadow *into that plane* — its projection (grey, dashed). Subtracting the shadow leaves the residual $\\mathbf v_3$ (purple), which sticks straight out of the plane, perpendicular to both $\\mathbf q_1$ and $\\mathbf q_2$. Normalizing gives $\\mathbf q_3$ and completes an orthonormal basis of all of $\\mathbb R^3$."
q1 = np.array([1.0, 0.0, 0.0])
q2 = np.array([0.0, 1.0, 0.0])
a3 = np.array([0.9, 0.6, 1.3])
shadow = (q1 @ a3)*q1 + (q2 @ a3)*q2     # projection onto the q1-q2 plane
v3 = a3 - shadow

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6.4, 5.2))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection="3d")
# the spanned plane (z=0)
gx, gy = np.meshgrid(np.linspace(-0.3, 1.3, 2), np.linspace(-0.3, 1.1, 2))
ax.plot_surface(gx, gy, np.zeros_like(gx), alpha=0.18, color=GREY)

def arr3(ax, v, color, label, lw=2.4):
    ax.quiver(0, 0, 0, v[0], v[1], v[2], color=color, lw=lw, arrow_length_ratio=0.12)
    ax.text(v[0]*1.05, v[1]*1.05, v[2]*1.05, label, color=color, fontsize=11)

arr3(ax, q1, ARROW, r"$\mathbf{q}_1$")
arr3(ax, q2, ACCENT, r"$\mathbf{q}_2$")
arr3(ax, a3, POINT, r"$\mathbf{a}_3$")
arr3(ax, shadow, GREY, "shadow", lw=1.8)
ax.plot([shadow[0], a3[0]], [shadow[1], a3[1]], [shadow[2], a3[2]],
        color=PURPLE, ls=":", lw=1.8)
arr3(ax, v3, PURPLE, r"$\mathbf{v}_3$")
ax.set_xlim(0, 1.3); ax.set_ylim(0, 1.1); ax.set_zlim(0, 1.4)
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("$y$"); ax.set_zlabel("$z$")
ax.set_title("Subtract the planar shadow; the leftover pokes out")
ax.view_init(elev=22, azim=-60)
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"In one sentence, what is Gram–Schmidt doing geometrically?"* It is repeatedly applying
the orthogonal decomposition of GEOM-6 — split each new vector into *the part I already
have* plus *the part that is new* — and keeping only the new part. The "part I already
have" is a projection onto the span built so far; the "new part" is the residual, which is
orthogonal to that span by construction. That is the entire algorithm; everything below is
making "the part I already have" precise and tallying the receipts.
:::

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

We now make the picture exact. We will need exactly one imported ingredient, the
projection of GEOM-6, so we restate it precisely before using it.

## The one tool we import: projection onto a unit vector {#sec-import}

From [GEOM-6], the **orthogonal projection** of a vector $\mathbf v$ onto the line spanned
by a nonzero vector $\mathbf a$ is

$$
\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf a}(\mathbf v)
= \frac{\mathbf a^\top\mathbf v}{\mathbf a^\top\mathbf a}\,\mathbf a,
$$ {#eq-proj-general}

and the defining guarantee that makes the whole process work is that the **residual**
$\mathbf v - \operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf a}(\mathbf v)$ is **orthogonal to $\mathbf a$**.
We use a sharpened form of this. When $\mathbf q$ is a *unit* vector ($\mathbf
q^\top\mathbf q = 1$), the denominator in @eq-proj-general is $1$, and the projection
simplifies to

$$
\operatorname{proj}_{\mathbf q}(\mathbf v) = (\mathbf q^\top\mathbf v)\,\mathbf q .
$$ {#eq-proj-unit}

The scalar $\mathbf q^\top\mathbf v$ is *how much of $\mathbf v$ points along $\mathbf q$*;
multiplying it back by $\mathbf q$ rebuilds the shadow as an actual vector. Since
Gram–Schmidt normalizes each direction *before* using it to project, we will always be in
this clean unit-vector case — that is precisely why we normalize as we go rather than at
the end.

We also need the residual-orthogonality guarantee in unit form, and we *prove* it inline
rather than merely cite it, so the chain is closed. Let $\mathbf r = \mathbf v -
(\mathbf q^\top\mathbf v)\,\mathbf q$ with $\mathbf q^\top\mathbf q = 1$. Then

$$
\mathbf q^\top\mathbf r
= \mathbf q^\top\mathbf v - (\mathbf q^\top\mathbf v)\,\mathbf q^\top\mathbf q
= \mathbf q^\top\mathbf v - (\mathbf q^\top\mathbf v)(1)
= 0 .
$$ {#eq-resid-orth}

Here we used that $\mathbf q^\top\mathbf v$ is a *scalar*, so it pulls out of the dot
product, and $\mathbf q^\top\mathbf q = 1$. So the leftover is genuinely perpendicular to
$\mathbf q$, no matter what $\mathbf v$ was. This single identity, used $k-1$ times at step
$k$, is the engine of everything that follows.

## The single-subtraction case, written out {#sec-two-step}

Take two linearly independent vectors $\mathbf a_1,\mathbf a_2 \in \mathbb R^m$. We build
$\mathbf q_1,\mathbf q_2$.

**Step 1 — the first direction.** There is no "span so far" to subtract from, so we just
keep $\mathbf a_1$'s direction. Define the unnormalized $\mathbf v_1 = \mathbf a_1$, its
length $r_{11} = \lVert\mathbf v_1\rVert$, and

$$
\mathbf q_1 = \frac{\mathbf v_1}{r_{11}} = \frac{\mathbf a_1}{\lVert\mathbf a_1\rVert}.
$$ {#eq-q1}

(Since the $\mathbf a_i$ are independent, none is the zero vector, so $r_{11} > 0$ and the
division is legal — we revisit what happens if independence fails in @sec-failure.) By
construction $\mathbf q_1^\top\mathbf q_1 = 1$ and $\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf q_1\} =
\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf a_1\}$, matching @eq-nested-span at $k=1$.

**Step 2 — subtract the shadow, keep the residual.** Form the residual of $\mathbf a_2$
after removing its projection onto $\mathbf q_1$, using @eq-proj-unit:

$$
\mathbf v_2 = \mathbf a_2 - (\mathbf q_1^\top\mathbf a_2)\,\mathbf q_1 .
$$ {#eq-v2}

By @eq-resid-orth (with $\mathbf v = \mathbf a_2$, $\mathbf q = \mathbf q_1$), we have
$\mathbf q_1^\top\mathbf v_2 = 0$: the residual is perpendicular to $\mathbf q_1$, exactly
as the picture promised. Is $\mathbf v_2$ nonzero? Yes — if it were the zero vector, then
$\mathbf a_2 = (\mathbf q_1^\top\mathbf a_2)\mathbf q_1$ would be a scalar multiple of
$\mathbf q_1$, hence of $\mathbf a_1$, contradicting that $\mathbf a_1,\mathbf a_2$ are
independent. So we may normalize:

$$
r_{22} = \lVert\mathbf v_2\rVert > 0,
\qquad
\mathbf q_2 = \frac{\mathbf v_2}{r_{22}}.
$$ {#eq-q2}

Now $\{\mathbf q_1,\mathbf q_2\}$ is orthonormal. And the span is preserved: $\mathbf q_2$
is a combination of $\mathbf a_2$ and $\mathbf q_1$ (which is a multiple of $\mathbf a_1$),
so $\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf q_1,\mathbf q_2\} \subseteq \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf
a_1,\mathbf a_2\}$; both are two-dimensional (two independent vectors each), so the
inclusion is equality — @eq-nested-span at $k=2$.

Already we can read off the coefficients we will need later. Rearranging @eq-v2 and
@eq-q2 to express the *originals* in terms of the *orthonormal* vectors:

$$
\mathbf a_1 = r_{11}\,\mathbf q_1,
\qquad
\mathbf a_2 = (\mathbf q_1^\top\mathbf a_2)\,\mathbf q_1 + r_{22}\,\mathbf q_2 .
$$ {#eq-a-in-q-2}

Notice that $\mathbf a_1$ uses only $\mathbf q_1$, while $\mathbf a_2$ uses $\mathbf q_1$
and $\mathbf q_2$. Each original column reaches back only to the orthonormal vectors with
*equal or smaller index*. Hold that thought — it is the triangularity of @sec-qr, already
visible.

## The general step and the full process {#sec-general}

Now the inductive step, which is @eq-v2 with more terms to subtract. Suppose we have
already produced orthonormal $\mathbf q_1,\dots,\mathbf q_{k-1}$ with
$\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf q_1,\dots,\mathbf q_{k-1}\} = \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf
a_1,\dots,\mathbf a_{k-1}\}$. To bring in $\mathbf a_k$, subtract its projection onto each
$\mathbf q_i$ already built. Because the $\mathbf q_i$ are *mutually* orthogonal, the
projection of $\mathbf a_k$ onto their whole span is just the **sum** of the one-
dimensional projections (this is the orthonormal-basis collapse from GEOM-5; we re-derive
its orthogonality below). Define the projection coefficients

$$
r_{ik} = \mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf a_k \qquad (i = 1,\dots,k-1),
$$ {#eq-rik}

and form the residual

$$
\mathbf v_k = \mathbf a_k - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} r_{ik}\,\mathbf q_i
            = \mathbf a_k - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} (\mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf a_k)\,\mathbf q_i .
$$ {#eq-vk}

::: {#lem-gs}
**Lemma (the residual is orthogonal to all earlier directions).** With $\mathbf v_k$ as in
@eq-vk and $\{\mathbf q_1,\dots,\mathbf q_{k-1}\}$ orthonormal, $\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf
v_k = 0$ for every $j = 1,\dots,k-1$.
:::

::: {.proof}
Fix any $j \in \{1,\dots,k-1\}$ and dot $\mathbf q_j$ into @eq-vk, distributing over the
sum:

$$
\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf v_k
= \mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf a_k
  - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} (\mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf a_k)\,(\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf q_i).
$$

The scalars $\mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf a_k$ pulled out of each dot product. Now use
orthonormality @eq-orthonormal: $\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf q_i = 0$ unless $i = j$, and
equals $1$ when $i = j$. Every term of the sum therefore dies except the single $i = j$
term, which contributes $(\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf a_k)(1)$:

$$
\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf v_k
= \mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf a_k - (\mathbf q_j^\top\mathbf a_k)
= 0 .
$$

This holds for each $j$, so $\mathbf v_k$ is orthogonal to all of $\mathbf q_1,\dots,\mathbf
q_{k-1}$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

That the residual is nonzero follows from independence, exactly as in @sec-two-step: if
$\mathbf v_k = \mathbf 0$ then $\mathbf a_k = \sum_{i<k} r_{ik}\mathbf q_i$ would lie in
$\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf q_1,\dots,\mathbf q_{k-1}\} = \operatorname{span}\{\mathbf
a_1,\dots,\mathbf a_{k-1}\}$, making $\mathbf a_1,\dots,\mathbf a_k$ dependent —
contradiction. So we normalize:

$$
r_{kk} = \lVert\mathbf v_k\rVert > 0,
\qquad
\mathbf q_k = \frac{\mathbf v_k}{r_{kk}}.
$$ {#eq-qk}

Now $\{\mathbf q_1,\dots,\mathbf q_k\}$ is orthonormal ($\mathbf q_k$ is orthogonal to the
earlier ones by @lem-gs, and has unit length by @eq-qk), and the span is preserved by the
same dimension argument as before. Induction from the $k=1$ base case @eq-q1 carries this
to $k = n$. We have proved the central theorem.

::: {#thm-gs}
**Theorem (Gram–Schmidt).** Let $\mathbf a_1,\dots,\mathbf a_n \in \mathbb R^m$ be linearly
independent. The vectors produced by

$$
\mathbf v_k = \mathbf a_k - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} (\mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf a_k)\,\mathbf q_i,
\qquad
\mathbf q_k = \frac{\mathbf v_k}{\lVert\mathbf v_k\rVert}
\qquad (k = 1,\dots,n)
$$

are orthonormal and satisfy $\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf q_1,\dots,\mathbf q_k\} =
\operatorname{span}\{\mathbf a_1,\dots,\mathbf a_k\}$ for every $k$.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Does the result depend on the order of the input vectors?"* Yes, and that is worth
saying out loud. Reorder the $\mathbf a_i$ and you generally get a *different* orthonormal
basis, because $\mathbf q_1$ always points along whichever vector you fed first, and every
later vector is orthogonalized against that choice. All such bases span the same final
space (@eq-nested-span holds for whatever order you pick), but they are different bases.
The *nested* spans, however, do depend on order — that is exactly the information the
triangular $R$ encodes. Practically, the ordering also affects numerical accuracy: feeding
the vectors so that nearly-dependent ones come later tends to be safer.
:::

## The QR decomposition: Gram–Schmidt, written as a matrix equation {#sec-qr}

Now we collect the receipts. We never threw any information away — every $\mathbf a_k$ was
*rebuilt* from the $\mathbf q_i$ with known coefficients. Rearrange @eq-vk and @eq-qk to
solve for the original $\mathbf a_k$. From @eq-qk, $\mathbf v_k = r_{kk}\mathbf q_k$;
substituting into @eq-vk and moving the sum to the other side,

$$
\mathbf a_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} r_{ik}\,\mathbf q_i + r_{kk}\,\mathbf q_k
            = \sum_{i=1}^{k} r_{ik}\,\mathbf q_i .
$$ {#eq-a-in-q}

(The first $k-1$ terms are the shadow we subtracted; the last term is the rebuilt
residual.) This says each original column is a combination of the orthonormal vectors with
index $\le k$ — *no $\mathbf q_i$ with $i > k$ appears*. Set $r_{ik} = 0$ for $i > k$, and
@eq-a-in-q holds with the sum running all the way to $n$:

$$
\mathbf a_k = \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_{ik}\,\mathbf q_i,
\qquad\text{with } r_{ik} = 0 \text{ whenever } i > k .
$$ {#eq-a-full}

This is exactly a matrix product. Stack the originals as the columns of
$A = [\,\mathbf a_1 \mid \dots \mid \mathbf a_n\,]$ ($m\times n$), the orthonormal vectors
as the columns of $Q = [\,\mathbf q_1 \mid \dots \mid \mathbf q_n\,]$ ($m\times n$), and
the coefficients as the entries of $R$ ($n\times n$) with $R_{ik} = r_{ik}$. The rule "the
$k$-th column of $A$ is the combination of $Q$'s columns weighted by the $k$-th column of
$R$" is precisely the definition of matrix multiplication $A = QR$ (the column view of
$Ax$ from [GEOM-8], applied column by column). The condition $r_{ik} = 0$ for $i > k$ means
**$R$ is upper-triangular**.

::: {#thm-qr}
**Theorem (QR decomposition).** Every real $m\times n$ matrix $A$ with linearly independent
columns ($m \ge n$) factors as

$$
A = QR,
$$ {#eq-qr}

where $Q$ is $m\times n$ with orthonormal columns ($Q^\top Q = I_n$) and $R$ is $n\times n$
upper-triangular with positive diagonal entries $r_{kk} = \lVert\mathbf v_k\rVert > 0$. The
columns of $Q$ and the entries of $R$ are exactly those produced by Gram–Schmidt
(@thm-gs). With the positive-diagonal convention, the factorization is **unique**.
:::

::: {.proof}
*Existence* is the construction: Gram–Schmidt produces $Q$ and the coefficients $r_{ik}$,
and @eq-a-full rearranges to @eq-qr. The diagonal entries are the norms $r_{kk} =
\lVert\mathbf v_k\rVert$, which we showed are strictly positive. We verify $Q^\top Q = I_n$
directly: the $(i,j)$ entry of $Q^\top Q$ is $\mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf q_j$, which is $1$ on
the diagonal and $0$ off it by orthonormality @eq-orthonormal — that is the identity
matrix.

*Uniqueness.* Suppose $A = Q_1R_1 = Q_2R_2$ with both factors of the stated form. We show
the factors coincide column by column. Equate the first columns: $\mathbf a_1 = (R_1)_{11}
\mathbf q^{(1)}_1 = (R_2)_{11}\mathbf q^{(2)}_1$. Taking norms and using that each $\mathbf
q$ is a unit vector, $(R_1)_{11} = \lVert\mathbf a_1\rVert = (R_2)_{11}$ (both positive by
convention), hence $\mathbf q^{(1)}_1 = \mathbf q^{(2)}_1$. Proceed by induction: having
matched the first $k-1$ columns of $Q$ and $R$, the $k$-th column gives $\mathbf a_k =
\sum_{i=1}^{k} (R)_{ik}\mathbf q_i$. The coefficients with $i < k$ are forced, because
dotting both sides with the (now-agreed) $\mathbf q_i$ isolates $(R)_{ik} = \mathbf
q_i^\top\mathbf a_k$. What remains, $\mathbf a_k - \sum_{i<k}(R)_{ik}\mathbf q_i =
(R)_{kk}\mathbf q_k$, then fixes $(R)_{kk} > 0$ as its norm and $\mathbf q_k$ as the unit
vector in its direction — identically for both factorizations. So $Q_1 = Q_2$ and $R_1 =
R_2$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Thin vs. full QR — a naming clarification you will meet"}
What Gram–Schmidt produces is the **thin** (or **reduced**) QR: $Q$ is $m\times n$ with
just enough orthonormal columns to span the column space of $A$. Libraries can also return
the **full** QR, where $Q$ is enlarged to a square $m\times m$ orthogonal matrix (its extra
$m-n$ columns are an orthonormal basis for the orthogonal complement — the *left null
space*, [GEOM-12]) and $R$ gains $m-n$ rows of zeros at the bottom. The two carry the same
information; the thin form is what least squares needs and what this article builds.
:::

## Why $Q$ makes projection trivial: $P = QQ^\top$ {#sec-projection}

Here is the immediate payoff that connects straight back to the keystone. In [GEOM-6] the
projection matrix onto the column space of a general $A$ was

$$
P = A(A^\top A)^{-1}A^\top,
$$ {#eq-P-general}

with that awkward inverse $(A^\top A)^{-1}$ sitting in the middle — the price of the
columns of $A$ being oblique. Replace $A$ by an orthonormal $Q$ and watch the inverse
evaporate. First, $Q^\top Q = I_n$ from @thm-qr, so $(Q^\top Q)^{-1} = I_n^{-1} = I_n$.
Substituting $Q$ for $A$ in @eq-P-general:

$$
P = Q(Q^\top Q)^{-1}Q^\top = Q\,I_n\,Q^\top = QQ^\top .
$$ {#eq-P-Q}

The projection onto the column space of $A$ — *the same subspace*, since $Q$ and $A$ have
the same span — is therefore the clean

$$
\boxed{\,P = QQ^\top\,.}
$$ {#eq-PQQt}

Read what $P\mathbf x = QQ^\top\mathbf x$ does, right to left, to see why this is the GEOM-5
coordinate collapse in disguise. The vector $Q^\top\mathbf x$ stacks the dot products
$\mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf x$ — the *coordinates of $\mathbf x$ along each orthonormal
direction*. Multiplying by $Q$ rebuilds the vector $\sum_i (\mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf
x)\mathbf q_i$ — *sum the shadows back up*. So projecting onto a subspace, once you hold an
orthonormal basis for it, is "measure each coordinate, add the pieces back" — no system to
solve. This is exactly why a least-squares solver reaches for QR (@sec-solving): it buys
the orthonormal basis once, then everything downstream is dot products.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
$QQ^\top$ and $Q^\top Q$ are **not** the same matrix and not interchangeable. $Q^\top Q =
I_n$ is the small $n\times n$ identity — it says the *columns* of $Q$ are orthonormal.
$QQ^\top$ is the $m\times m$ **projection** matrix onto the column space — and it equals
the identity $I_m$ only when $Q$ is square (i.e. its columns are a full basis of all of
$\mathbb R^m$). When $n < m$, $QQ^\top \neq I_m$; it is a genuine projector with $QQ^\top
\mathbf x = \mathbf x$ only for $\mathbf x$ already in the column space. Confusing the two
is one of the most common QR errors.
:::

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We run the process on real, honestly-shaped data. The first we carry through **by hand,
number by number**; the second is the workhorse application — least squares — on a genuine
dataset; the third walks straight to the edge of applicability where the *classical*
process fails on a computer.

## Example 1 — orthonormalizing three feature vectors by hand {#sec-ex-byhand}

Three measured "feature" vectors in $\mathbb R^4$ (think four observations of three
correlated predictors). They are independent but oblique — the typical raw material. We
orthonormalize them and read off $Q$ and $R$, checking every claim.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-ex1-data
#| echo: false
A = np.array([
    [ 1.0,  1.0,  2.0],
    [ 1.0,  2.0,  0.0],
    [ 0.0,  1.0,  1.0],
    [ 1.0,  0.0,  1.0],
])
a1, a2, a3 = A[:, 0], A[:, 1], A[:, 2]

# --- step 1 ---
r11 = np.linalg.norm(a1)
q1 = a1 / r11
# --- step 2 ---
r12 = q1 @ a2
v2 = a2 - r12 * q1
r22 = np.linalg.norm(v2)
q2 = v2 / r22
# --- step 3 ---
r13 = q1 @ a3
r23 = q2 @ a3
v3 = a3 - r13 * q1 - r23 * q2
r33 = np.linalg.norm(v3)
q3 = v3 / r33

Q = np.column_stack([q1, q2, q3])
R = np.array([[r11, r12, r13],
              [0.0, r22, r23],
              [0.0, 0.0, r33]])

np.set_printoptions(precision=4, suppress=True)
print("A =\n", A)
print(f"\nStep 1:  r11 = ||a1||      = {r11:.4f}")
print("         q1 = a1 / r11   =", np.round(q1, 4))
print(f"\nStep 2:  r12 = q1 . a2     = {r12:.4f}")
print("         v2 = a2 - r12 q1 =", np.round(v2, 4))
print(f"         r22 = ||v2||     = {r22:.4f}")
print("         q2 = v2 / r22    =", np.round(q2, 4))
print(f"\nStep 3:  r13 = q1 . a3     = {r13:.4f}    r23 = q2 . a3 = {r23:.4f}")
print("         v3 = a3 - r13 q1 - r23 q2 =", np.round(v3, 4))
print(f"         r33 = ||v3||     = {r33:.4f}")
print("         q3 = v3 / r33    =", np.round(q3, 4))
```

Walk the printed arithmetic against the formulas:

- **Step 1** keeps $\mathbf a_1$'s direction: $r_{11} = \lVert\mathbf a_1\rVert =
  \sqrt{1^2+1^2+0^2+1^2} = \sqrt 3 = `{python} f"{r11:.4f}"`$, and $\mathbf q_1 = \mathbf
  a_1/r_{11}$ — @eq-q1.
- **Step 2** subtracts the shadow of $\mathbf a_2$ on $\mathbf q_1$: the coefficient is
  $r_{12} = \mathbf q_1^\top\mathbf a_2 = `{python} f"{r12:.4f}"`$, the residual is $\mathbf
  v_2 = \mathbf a_2 - r_{12}\mathbf q_1$ — @eq-v2 — and we normalize by $r_{22} = `{python}
  f"{r22:.4f}"`$.
- **Step 3** subtracts the shadow of $\mathbf a_3$ on the *whole* $\mathbf q_1,\mathbf q_2$
  plane: two coefficients $r_{13} = `{python} f"{r13:.4f}"`$, $r_{23} = `{python}
  f"{r23:.4f}"`$, residual $\mathbf v_3 = \mathbf a_3 - r_{13}\mathbf q_1 - r_{23}\mathbf
  q_2$ — @eq-vk — normalized by $r_{33} = `{python} f"{r33:.4f}"`$.

Now verify every claim of @thm-qr numerically: $Q^\top Q$ should be the identity, $R$
should be upper-triangular, and $QR$ should reproduce $A$ exactly.

```{python}
#| label: ex1-checks
print("Q^T Q  (should be I_3):\n", np.round(Q.T @ Q, 6))
print("\nR  (should be upper-triangular, positive diagonal):\n", np.round(R, 4))
print("\nQ R  (should equal A):\n", np.round(Q @ R, 6))
print("\nmax |QR - A| =", np.max(np.abs(Q @ R - A)))
# compare against the library's QR (signs of columns may differ by convention)
Qnp, Rnp = np.linalg.qr(A)
print("\nnumpy diagonal of R:", np.round(np.diag(Rnp), 4),
      "  ours:", np.round(np.diag(R), 4))
```

The off-diagonal entries of $Q^\top Q$ are zero to machine precision, the diagonal is all
ones, $R$ is upper-triangular with a positive diagonal, and $QR$ rebuilds $A$ to $\sim
10^{-15}$. The factorization is real and exact. (NumPy's $R$ may differ from ours by signs
along the diagonal — a column-sign convention, not a different factorization; with the
positive-diagonal convention of @thm-qr they agree.) @fig-ex1-gram visualizes the payoff:
the **Gram matrix** $A^\top A$ of pairwise column dot products is dense and off-diagonal-
heavy (the columns are tangled), while $Q^\top Q$ is the clean identity (the columns are
untangled).

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex1-gram
#| fig-cap: "What orthonormalization buys, seen as heatmaps of pairwise dot products. **Left:** the Gram matrix $A^\\top A$ of the original columns — big off-diagonal entries mean the features overlap and their coordinates are coupled. **Right:** $Q^\\top Q = I$ — every off-diagonal dot product is zero, so the new coordinates are completely decoupled. Gram–Schmidt is the transformation from the left picture to the right while preserving the span."
fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.4, 3.7))
G = A.T @ A
im1 = axL.imshow(G, cmap="PuOr", vmin=-G.max(), vmax=G.max())
axL.set_title(r"$A^\top A$ (columns overlap)")
for (i, j), val in np.ndenumerate(G):
    axL.text(j, i, f"{val:.1f}", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=10)
plt.colorbar(im1, ax=axL, fraction=0.046)

QtQ = Q.T @ Q
im2 = axR.imshow(QtQ, cmap="PuOr", vmin=-1, vmax=1)
axR.set_title(r"$Q^\top Q = I$ (decoupled)")
for (i, j), val in np.ndenumerate(QtQ):
    axR.text(j, i, f"{val:.1f}", ha="center", va="center", fontsize=10)
plt.colorbar(im2, ax=axR, fraction=0.046)
for ax in (axL, axR):
    ax.set_xticks([0, 1, 2]); ax.set_yticks([0, 1, 2])
plt.tight_layout()
```

## Example 2 — least squares the stable way: QR vs. the normal equations {#sec-ex-leastsq}

This is the application that earns QR its place in the track. We fit a line to a genuine
physical dataset — **car stopping distance vs. speed**, from the classic 1920s `cars`
measurements (50 cars, speed in mph, stopping distance in feet) — and solve the least-
squares problem *two* ways: by forming and inverting $X^\top X$ (the normal equations of
[REGR-1]), and by QR. The point is not the line itself but *why QR is the better road to
it*.

The least-squares problem is: find $\boldsymbol\beta$ minimizing $\lVert\mathbf y -
X\boldsymbol\beta\rVert^2$, where $X = [\,\mathbf 1 \mid \text{speed}\,]$ is the design
matrix (a column of ones for the intercept, a column of speeds for the slope). From
[GEOM-6]/[REGR-1] the solution satisfies the normal equations $X^\top X\boldsymbol\beta =
X^\top\mathbf y$. Substitute $X = QR$ and simplify:

$$
X^\top X\boldsymbol\beta = X^\top\mathbf y
\;\Longrightarrow\;
(QR)^\top(QR)\boldsymbol\beta = (QR)^\top\mathbf y
\;\Longrightarrow\;
R^\top\underbrace{Q^\top Q}_{=\,I}R\,\boldsymbol\beta = R^\top Q^\top\mathbf y .
$$

Here $(QR)^\top = R^\top Q^\top$ (the transpose reverses a product — [GEOM-10]) and $Q^\top
Q = I$. The left side is $R^\top R\boldsymbol\beta$. Since $R$ is upper-triangular with
positive diagonal it is invertible, so $R^\top$ is too; multiply both sides by
$(R^\top)^{-1}$ to cancel it:

$$
R\,\boldsymbol\beta = Q^\top\mathbf y .
$$ {#eq-qr-solve}

This is a triangular system — solvable by **back-substitution** (bottom row gives the last
unknown directly, then work upward), no matrix inverse at all. We never formed $X^\top X$;
that matters because, as the numbers will show and [GEOM-26] will prove, forming $X^\top X$
*squares the conditioning* of the problem, throwing away half your significant digits
before you even solve.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex2-leastsq
#| fig-cap: "Least squares on the `cars` dataset (stopping distance vs. speed, $n=50$), solved via QR. **Left:** the fitted line $\\hat y = R^{-1}Q^\\top\\mathbf y$ through the cloud. **Right:** residuals vs. speed — a roughly flat band (with the mild fan of real braking data), confirming a line is an honest first summary. The printed block shows the QR solution agrees with the normal-equation solution to many digits *here*, where the problem is well-conditioned; Example 3 and GEOM-26 show where they diverge."
# The classic R `cars` dataset (speed in mph, dist in ft), entered explicitly.
speed = np.array([4,4,7,7,8,9,10,10,10,11,11,12,12,12,12,13,13,13,13,14,14,14,14,
                  15,15,15,16,16,17,17,17,18,18,18,18,19,19,19,20,20,20,20,20,22,
                  23,24,24,24,24,25], dtype=float)
dist  = np.array([2,10,4,22,16,10,18,26,34,17,28,14,20,24,28,26,34,34,46,26,36,60,
                  80,20,26,54,32,40,32,40,50,42,56,76,84,36,46,68,32,48,52,56,64,
                  66,54,70,92,93,120,85], dtype=float)
n = len(speed)
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), speed])    # design matrix [1 | speed]
y = dist

# --- route A: normal equations (form and invert X^T X) ---
beta_normal = np.linalg.inv(X.T @ X) @ (X.T @ y)
# --- route B: QR + back-substitution ---
Qx, Rx = gram_schmidt(X)                     # our own Gram–Schmidt
rhs = Qx.T @ y
beta_qr = np.zeros(2)
for i in (1, 0):                              # back-substitution
    beta_qr[i] = (rhs[i] - Rx[i, i+1:] @ beta_qr[i+1:]) / Rx[i, i]

xs = np.linspace(speed.min(), speed.max(), 100)
fitted = X @ beta_qr
resid = y - fitted

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 3.7))
axL.scatter(speed, dist, color=POINT, s=28, alpha=0.85, label="cars")
axL.plot(xs, beta_qr[0] + beta_qr[1]*xs, color=ARROW,
         label=fr"$\hat y = {beta_qr[0]:.2f} + {beta_qr[1]:.2f}\,x$")
axL.set_xlabel("speed (mph)"); axL.set_ylabel("stopping distance (ft)")
axL.set_title("Least-squares fit via QR"); axL.legend(loc="upper left", fontsize=9)

axR.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=1.2)
axR.scatter(speed, resid, color=POINT, s=28, alpha=0.85)
axR.set_xlabel("speed (mph)"); axR.set_ylabel("residual")
axR.set_title("Residuals vs. speed")
plt.tight_layout()

print("beta via normal equations :", np.round(beta_normal, 6))
print("beta via QR + back-subst   :", np.round(beta_qr, 6))
print("max difference             :", np.max(np.abs(beta_normal - beta_qr)))
print("cond(X)     =", f"{np.linalg.cond(X):.1f}")
print("cond(X^T X) =", f"{np.linalg.cond(X.T @ X):.1f}",
      "  <-- the normal equations work with THIS, the SQUARE of cond(X)")
```

Both routes land on the same line here, because this problem is mild. But read the last
two printed numbers: $\operatorname{cond}(X^\top X) = \operatorname{cond}(X)^2$. The
condition number measures how much a solve can amplify error ([GEOM-26]); squaring it is
the normal equations' hidden tax. On a well-conditioned problem like `cars` the tax is
affordable; on an ill-conditioned one it is ruinous, and that is exactly what Example 3
exhibits.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"If both give the same answer, why prefer QR over the normal equations?"* On paper they
are algebraically identical — @eq-qr-solve was *derived from* the normal equations. The
difference is purely numerical and it is large. Forming $X^\top X$ squares the condition
number, so a design matrix that loses (say) 4 digits of accuracy in a QR solve loses 8 in
the normal-equation solve — and if you only had 16 to begin with, that can be the
difference between a usable answer and noise. QR works with $X$ directly and never builds
the worse-conditioned $X^\top X$. The rule of thumb you should be able to state: **"for
least squares, factor the design matrix (QR or SVD); don't form and invert $X^\top X$."**
The SVD ([GEOM-20]) is the even-more-robust option for the genuinely rank-deficient case.
:::

## Example 3 — the edge of applicability: classical Gram–Schmidt loses orthogonality {#sec-ex-edge}

Here is the honest boundary, the case the technique strains and a naive implementation
breaks. We build a matrix whose columns are *nearly* dependent — almost collinear, the
numerical shadow of the multicollinearity that haunts regression — using a **Hilbert-like**
construction known to be brutally ill-conditioned. We then run *classical* Gram–Schmidt
(the formula of @thm-gs, projecting each original column against the finished $\mathbf q$'s)
and measure how badly the supposedly-orthonormal $Q$ fails to be orthonormal.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ex3-loss
#| fig-cap: "The edge of applicability: loss of orthogonality on an ill-conditioned matrix. For Hilbert matrices of growing size (rising condition number, x-axis log scale), we measure $\\lVert Q^\\top Q - I\\rVert$ — how far the computed $Q$ is from truly orthonormal. **Classical** Gram–Schmidt (red) degrades catastrophically: by $\\kappa\\sim10^{10}$ its $Q$ has essentially lost orthogonality. **Modified** Gram–Schmidt (green) — the same arithmetic, one reordering — holds far longer. This is the first hard evidence for the numerical-stability theme that closes the track (GEOM-26)."
from scipy.linalg import hilbert

sizes = range(2, 13)
conds, loss_cls, loss_mod = [], [], []
for nn in sizes:
    H = hilbert(nn)                       # famously ill-conditioned, columns near-dependent
    conds.append(np.linalg.cond(H))
    Qc, _ = gram_schmidt(H, modified=False)
    Qm, _ = gram_schmidt(H, modified=True)
    I = np.eye(nn)
    loss_cls.append(np.linalg.norm(Qc.T @ Qc - I))
    loss_mod.append(np.linalg.norm(Qm.T @ Qm - I))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.2))
ax.loglog(conds, loss_cls, "o-", color=ARROW, label="classical Gram–Schmidt")
ax.loglog(conds, loss_mod, "s-", color=ACCENT, label="modified Gram–Schmidt")
ax.axhline(1.0, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.0)
ax.text(conds[1], 1.3, "total loss of orthogonality", color=GREY, fontsize=9)
ax.set_xlabel(r"condition number $\kappa(H)$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$\|Q^\top Q - I\|$  (orthogonality error)")
ax.set_title("Classical Gram–Schmidt decays; modified holds")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()

print("size  cond(H)        classical err     modified err")
for nn, c, lc, lm in zip(sizes, conds, loss_cls, loss_mod):
    print(f"{nn:>3}   {c:.2e}      {lc:.2e}      {lm:.2e}")
```

The numbers are stark: as the columns approach dependence, classical Gram–Schmidt's $Q$
stops being orthonormal — $\lVert Q^\top Q - I\rVert$ climbs toward $1$ and beyond, meaning
the columns it produced are no longer perpendicular at all. Why does this happen, and why
does the modified version rescue it? That is the next section, and it is the article's
first real lesson in numerical reality.

# The numerical aside: classical vs. modified Gram–Schmidt {#sec-numerical}

The math above is exact; computers are not. We keep this secondary to the math — but it is
the honest reason practitioners do not implement @thm-gs literally.

## Where the error enters {#sec-error}

Look again at the general step @eq-vk. **Classical** Gram–Schmidt computes every
coefficient $r_{ik} = \mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf a_k$ against the *original* $\mathbf a_k$,
then subtracts all the shadows at once:

$$
\mathbf v_k = \mathbf a_k - (\mathbf q_1^\top\mathbf a_k)\mathbf q_1 - (\mathbf
q_2^\top\mathbf a_k)\mathbf q_2 - \dots - (\mathbf q_{k-1}^\top\mathbf a_k)\mathbf q_{k-1}.
$$

In exact arithmetic the $\mathbf q_i$ are perfectly orthonormal, so this is fine. But on a
computer the earlier $\mathbf q_i$ carry tiny rounding errors and are only *nearly*
orthonormal. When $\mathbf a_k$ is nearly in the span of the earlier vectors — the ill-
conditioned case — the true residual $\mathbf v_k$ is tiny, computed as the difference of
large nearly-equal quantities (**catastrophic cancellation**). The small errors in the
$\mathbf q_i$, which were harmless when $\mathbf v_k$ was large, now dominate the tiny true
residual, and the resulting $\mathbf q_k$ tilts away from perpendicular.

## The fix: subtract as you go {#sec-modified}

**Modified** Gram–Schmidt computes the *identical* quantity in exact arithmetic but in a
different order: instead of projecting the original $\mathbf a_k$ against each $\mathbf
q_i$, it subtracts each shadow immediately and projects the *running, partially-cleaned*
residual against the next direction. Writing $\mathbf v_k^{(0)} = \mathbf a_k$ and
updating,

$$
\mathbf v_k^{(i)} = \mathbf v_k^{(i-1)} - \bigl(\mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf v_k^{(i-1)}\bigr)\,
\mathbf q_i \qquad (i = 1,\dots,k-1),
\qquad \mathbf v_k = \mathbf v_k^{(k-1)} .
$$ {#eq-mgs}

Compare the coefficient: classical uses $\mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf a_k$ (against the
original); modified uses $\mathbf q_i^\top\mathbf v_k^{(i-1)}$ (against what is left after
the earlier subtractions). In exact arithmetic these are equal — once you have subtracted
the $\mathbf q_1,\dots,\mathbf q_{i-1}$ components, dotting with $\mathbf q_i$ gives the
same number either way, because $\mathbf q_i$ is orthogonal to the pieces already removed.
But in floating point, modified Gram–Schmidt projects against the freshly-cleaned residual,
so each subtraction also mops up the small errors left by the previous ones instead of
letting them accumulate. The reordering costs nothing extra in operations and buys the
dramatically better orthogonality you saw in green in @fig-ex3-loss. The `gram_schmidt`
helper at the top of this article implements both; the only difference between the two
branches is exactly @eq-vk versus @eq-mgs.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is modified Gram–Schmidt the most stable way to get a QR?"* It is much better than
classical, but not the best available. The gold standard is **Householder reflections**
(or **Givens rotations**), which build $Q$ from exact orthogonal transformations and are
*unconditionally* stable — the computed $Q$ is orthonormal to machine precision regardless
of conditioning. They are what LAPACK and hence `numpy.linalg.qr` actually use. Gram–
Schmidt's advantage is conceptual transparency (it *is* the projection picture) and that it
produces the $\mathbf q_i$ one at a time in order, which some iterative algorithms (Arnoldi,
GMRES) need. The honest summary: **understand the world through Gram–Schmidt; compute a
dense QR with Householder.** Householder is a topic for the numerical-methods material the
track points toward in [GEOM-26].
:::

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

The construction has a clean domain of validity; knowing its boundary is the difference
between using QR and misusing it.

## Linearly dependent columns: the process stalls {#sec-dependent}

@thm-gs assumes the input vectors are linearly independent, and that assumption is load-
bearing. If $\mathbf a_k$ lies in the span of $\mathbf a_1,\dots,\mathbf a_{k-1}$, then its
residual is $\mathbf v_k = \mathbf 0$ exactly (in exact arithmetic), $r_{kk} = \lVert\mathbf
v_k\rVert = 0$, and the normalization @eq-qk **divides by zero**. Geometrically this is
honest, not a bug: a dependent column contributes *no new direction*, so there is no new
$\mathbf q_k$ to manufacture. The remedies are real and worth naming:

- **Skip-and-continue** (rank-revealing QR): when $r_{kk} = 0$, drop that column, record
  the deficiency, and proceed. The result is a QR of the independent columns plus a
  recorded rank — exactly the rank computation of [GEOM-12].
- **Column pivoting** (`scipy.linalg.qr(..., pivoting=True)`): permute the most-independent
  column to the front at each step, which both handles near-dependence gracefully and
  reveals numerical rank.
- **Use the SVD** ([GEOM-20]) when the matrix is genuinely rank-deficient and you want the
  most stable possible handling — the SVD never divides by anything and reads rank straight
  off the singular values.

## Near-dependence: the silent danger {#sec-near-dependent}

Worse than exact dependence — which at least announces itself with a zero — is *near*
dependence, the case of @sec-ex-edge. Here $r_{kk}$ is small but nonzero, so the
normalization succeeds and returns a $\mathbf q_k$, but that $\mathbf q_k$ was computed by
dividing a noise-dominated residual by a tiny number, so it is essentially random. The
process gives an answer; the answer is wrong; nothing warns you. This is the numerical
fingerprint of **multicollinearity** in regression ([REGR-7]) and precisely what the
**condition number** ([GEOM-26]) quantifies. The defense is the same as above: modified
Gram–Schmidt buys you headroom, Householder buys more, column pivoting reveals the problem,
and the SVD handles it most gracefully.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
A successful run of Gram–Schmidt does **not** certify that your $Q$ is orthonormal. On
ill-conditioned input the algorithm completes happily and hands back columns that are
visibly *not* perpendicular (@fig-ex3-loss). Always sanity-check a hand-rolled QR by
computing $\lVert Q^\top Q - I\rVert$; if it is not near machine epsilon, your basis is a
fiction. This is why production code does not hand-roll Gram–Schmidt — it calls a
Householder-based library QR.
:::

## When QR is the right tool — and when to reach elsewhere {#sec-when}

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When do you use QR, and when something else?"* The honest practitioner's summary:

- **Use QR** to (a) manufacture an orthonormal basis for a given span, (b) solve full-rank
  least squares stably (@sec-ex-leastsq) — the default for well- to moderately-conditioned
  design matrices — and (c) feed iterative eigenvalue/Krylov methods that need orthonormal
  vectors produced in order.
- **Reach for the SVD** ([GEOM-20]–[GEOM-21]) instead when the matrix is rank-deficient or
  severely ill-conditioned, when you need the *minimum-norm* least-squares solution, or when
  you want low-rank approximation — the SVD is more expensive but maximally robust and
  reveals rank exactly.
- **Reach for Cholesky** ([GEOM-19]) when you genuinely want to solve a *symmetric positive-
  definite* system fast and the conditioning is benign — it is about twice as cheap as QR,
  at the cost of the squared-conditioning issue we flagged.
- **Don't** form and invert $X^\top X$ for least squares unless the problem is small and
  well-conditioned and you understand the cost; the squared condition number is a real
  hazard.
:::

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from the keystone **[GEOM-6 *Orthogonal Projection onto a Subspace*]**, whose
single move — project, keep the residual — this article ran in a disciplined loop to
manufacture orthonormal bases, and from **[GEOM-5 *Orthogonality and Orthonormal Bases*]**,
whose promise of "golden coordinate systems" we made constructive. From here:

- **[GEOM-8 *Matrices as Linear Maps*]** — the next trunk article; it makes precise the
  "columns of $A$ are a combination of $Q$'s columns" reading of $A=QR$ we used in
  @sec-qr, and the geometry of what $Q$ (an orthogonal map: a rotation/reflection) does to
  space.
- **[GEOM-11 *Solving $A\mathbf x = \mathbf b$*]** and **[GEOM-12 *Rank and the Four
  Subspaces*]** — where QR becomes a *solving* tool: the triangular system @eq-qr-solve and
  the rank-revealing variant of @sec-dependent.
- **[GEOM-20 *The Singular Value Decomposition*]** — the more powerful cousin that always
  exists (even for dependent columns), the tool @sec-when sends you to when QR strains.
- **[GEOM-26 *Condition Number and Numerical Stability*]** — pays off the orthogonality-
  loss story of @sec-ex-edge in full, proves why the normal equations square the
  conditioning, and ranks Gram–Schmidt against Householder.
- **Out to Regression:** this is the numerically sound least-squares route offered to
  **[REGR-1]** and the multicollinearity-aware tooling of **[REGR-7]** — QR is how a real
  regression library computes the line that REGR-1 derives by hand.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary sources.** E. Schmidt, "Zur Theorie der linearen und nichtlinearen
  Integralgleichungen" (*Math. Ann.*, 1907), where the iterative orthogonalization is
  stated cleanly; J. P. Gram's 1883 paper on series expansions by least squares, where the
  determinant-based version appears. The successive-orthogonalization idea predates both in
  Laplace and Cauchy.
- **The numerical-analysis classic.** Å. Björck, *Numerical Methods for Least Squares
  Problems* (SIAM, 1996), and Trefethen & Bau, *Numerical Linear Algebra* (SIAM, 1997),
  Lectures 7–8 and 10, for the loss-of-orthogonality analysis of @sec-numerical and the
  Householder alternative — the rigorous version of the picture in @fig-ex3-loss.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *Why exactly do Householder
  reflections beat Gram–Schmidt?* — the backward-stability argument, pointed to from
  @sec-modified, belongs with [GEOM-26]. *What if the matrix is rank-deficient by design?* —
  that is the pseudoinverse and the SVD, [GEOM-20]–[GEOM-21]. *Does $QR$ help compute
  eigenvalues?* — yes: the *QR algorithm* iterates this factorization to find eigenvalues,
  a beautiful payoff that lives with the eigenstructure articles [GEOM-14]–[GEOM-16].
